statement.markdown: Given the root of a binary tree, return the preorder traversal of its nodes' values. (i.e., from left to right, visit the root, then traverse the left subtree, then traverse the right subtree). You may use recursion or iteration.

constraints.description: The number of nodes in the tree.
The values of the nodes in the tree.

examples.explanation: The preorder traversal of the tree is root, left, right: 9, 2, 3.
The preorder traversal of a single node tree is the node itself.
The preorder traversal of a tree with a left child: 1, 2, 3.
The preorder traversal of a tree with a right child: 1, 2.

hints.text: Think about the order in which you visit the nodes in a preorder traversal. You need to visit the root node first, then its left subtree, and finally its right subtree.
Consider using a stack data structure to keep track of the nodes you need to visit. This will help you implement the traversal iteratively.

The key to solving this problem is to recognize that preorder traversal can be achieved by visiting the current node, then pushing its right child onto the stack (if it exists), and finally pushing its left child onto the stack (if it exists). This ensures that the left subtree is visited before the right subtree.

editorial.title: Preorder Traversal of a Tree
editorial.intuition: The problem requires traversing a tree in a preorder manner, which means visiting the root node first, then the left subtree, and finally the right subtree. This can be achieved using a stack to keep track of nodes to visit.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve recursively traversing the tree, but this can lead to a stack overflow for large trees. An iterative approach using a stack is more efficient.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach uses a stack to store nodes to visit. It starts by pushing the root node onto the stack. Then, it enters a loop where it pops a node from the stack, visits it, and pushes its right and left children onto the stack (if they exist). This ensures that nodes are visited in preorder.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution has a time complexity of O(n) and a space complexity of O(n), where n is the number of nodes in the tree. This is because each node is visited once and stored on the stack at most once.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by induction. The base case is when the tree is empty (i.e., the root is None), in which case the function returns an empty list. For a non-empty tree, the function visits the root node first, then recursively visits the left and right subtrees by pushing them onto the stack. The induction hypothesis is that the function correctly traverses trees with fewer nodes. Therefore, the function correctly traverses the entire tree in preorder.
editorial.dryRun:
A dry run of the optimal solution with a sample tree (e.g.,      1
     / \
    2   3
   / 
  4   5) confirms that it produces the correct preorder traversal: [1, 2, 4, 5, 3].
commonMistakes: Forgetting to push the right child onto the stack before the left child, which would result in a reverse preorder traversal.
Not handling the case where the tree is empty (i.e., the root is None).
Using a queue instead of a stack, which would result in a level-order traversal instead of a preorder traversal.
Not popping nodes from the stack when visiting them, which would result in an infinite loop.
Not checking if a node has children before pushing them onto the stack.
